item.title: Government disburses K630 million for fuel price subsidy
item.description: THE Government has disbursed K630 million as subsidy for the fuel price increase created by the Middle East conflict, an official says. Chairman of the advisory committee on the fuel crisis Joseph Lelang told Parliament yesterday that K247 million was released at the start of the price increase in April, and K383 million was released recently.
item.content:encoded:
THE Government has disbursed K630 million as subsidy for the fuel price increase created by the Middle East conflict, an official says.

Chairman of the advisory committee on the fuel crisis Joseph Lelang told Parliament yesterday that K247 million was released at the start of the price increase in April, and K383 million was released recently.

“The Government has not budgeted this amount but can reallocate funds within the existing budget,” Lelang said.

“The price stabilisation programme has successfully reduced fuel prices.

“The sustainability of the programme is being evaluated, with policy options being considered.

“The public is encouraged to report any price gouging to the ICCC (Independent Consumer and Competition Commission) for investigation and penalties.”

The committee has completed the review of policy options to sustain the K1 billion fuel subsidy programme, including assessing Treasury’s ability to reallocate budget resources, and bring a sustainability decision to Treasury for consideration.

“The Middle East war has not eased,” Lelang said.

“As of today, the spot price for crude oil is around US$93.50 (about K394) a barrel.

“Around April it was around US$139 (about K585) per barrel.

“It’s easing down but will continue to remain high for some time.

“So the issue of sustainability of the fuel price is very important, and the task force team, together with Treasury, are looking at options so that we can sustain the subsidy programme.

“If we can come up with certain sustainability options, we should be able to push that back towards the end of the year or so.

“And then we can look at what options are there, as the global well prices of crude oil continue to remain high.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Government admits issues in shift to SBC
item.description: THE Government has only provided Standard Based Curriculum (SBC) materials for Mathematics and Science from grades three to six since replacing the Outcome-Based Education (OBE), acting Education Minister Kinoka Feo said. He was responding in Parliament to Kabwum MP Patrick Basa who questioned the lack of teaching guides, syllabus and students’ textbooks for the SBC education system.
item.content:encoded:
THE Government has only provided Standard Based Curriculum (SBC) materials for Mathematics and Science from grades three to six since replacing the Outcome-Based Education (OBE), acting Education Minister Kinoka Feo said.

He was responding in Parliament to Kabwum MP Patrick Basa who questioned the lack of teaching guides, syllabus and students’ textbooks for the SBC education system.

Feo said producing curriculum materials for other subjects in all grades was costly. So the Government is rolling them out gradually.

He said the transition from SBC to OBE happened in 2015 with all teachers around the country undertaking the relevant training.

“In terms of curriculum, we have three basic components which are the teacher’s guides, teacher’s syllabus and students’ materials,” he said.

“We have delivered materials for the Mathematics and Science subjects for grade three to six only.”

Feo said that for other subjects, it would cost them around K6 million to produce the materials. So they are doing it slowly.

“By next year, the Government would release more funding for us to produce the materials,” he said

Basa noted that many teachers including those in his district were facing a lack of materials to teach.

The National earlier reported concerns raised by teachers on the lack of SBC materials which hindered teaching and learning in schools.

Some said that they had to spend their own resources to go online and get materials and information to teach their students.

It is wrong because examinations are set according to what is in the text books – not on the internet.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Charity group receives household goods, kitchen appliances
item.description: THE Kaugere Living Light Four-Square Gospel Church’s Moale Ministry will now be able to serve more children and more effectively after receiving a donation of household goods and kitchen appliances worth over K4,000 from BNBM PNG Limited. The donation, presented to the church yesterday, is aimed at upgrading the ministry’s facilities so volunteers can prepare meals faster and maintain better hygie
item.content:encoded:
THE Kaugere Living Light Four-Square Gospel Church’s Moale Ministry will now be able to serve more children and more effectively after receiving a donation of household goods and kitchen appliances worth over K4,000 from BNBM PNG Limited.

The donation, presented to the church yesterday, is aimed at upgrading the ministry’s facilities so volunteers can prepare meals faster and maintain better hygiene while hosting the weekly outreach for underprivileged children in Kaugere.

Moale Ministry runs every Thursday, bringing children together for gospel teaching, sports, and Bible verse games before providing a meal and arranging their return home.

With the new equipment, church leaders said the programme can expand its reach and improve the quality of care offered to the children who attend.

Church pastors Maria Kiagi and Dobi Tomuriesa said the donation directly addressed a long-standing need for proper kitchen facilities.

“This support means we can now cook and serve meals in a more organised and dignified way,” Ps Kiagi said.

“You are not wasting your resources through this donation, but you are investing in human lives.

“God above will repay you with countless blessings as you move your business forward,” she added.

Kiagi added that every Thursday, the ministry took care and fed up to a thousand children across the Moresby South electorate.

She said these new utensils and household goods would now help them do their work efficiently.

BNBM PNG representative Alysha Paisabua said the company chose to support the ministry because its mission aligns with BNBM’s motto, “Building a better future”.

“We commend the work the Moale Ministry is doing for children in our city.

“Providing the right tools makes it easier for them to focus on caring for the children rather than struggling with basic resources,” Paisabua said.

The donation marks BNBM PNG’s fourth contribution to community institutions in Port Moresby, following earlier support to Wardstrip Demonstration Primary School, the Obstetrics and Gynecology Ward at Port Moresby General Hospital, and Buk Bilong Pikinini.

Church officials said the upgraded kitchen capacity would allow the ministry to accommodate more children and reduce pressure on volunteers during Thursday programme.

BNBM PNG confirmed it plans to continue supporting community-based initiatives across the National Capital District.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Namatanai resumes court circuit hearings after years
item.description: NATIONAL Court circuit hearings have been welcomed in New Ireland’s Namatanai after years of absence. In recent years, National Court hearings had not been conducted in Namatanai or Lihir, with all cases being heard in Kavieng.
item.content:encoded:
NATIONAL Court circuit hearings have been welcomed in New Ireland’s Namatanai after years of absence.

In recent years, National Court hearings had not been conducted in Namatanai or Lihir, with all cases being heard in Kavieng.

This had forced residents to travel along the Buluminski Highway to access court services, increasing both costs and safety risks.

There is no resident National Court judge based in New Ireland and judicial services are delivered through visiting circuit courts.

During a recent sitting in Kavieng, visiting Judge Justice Panuel Mogish saw the absence of circuit hearings in Namatanai and moved to extend court sittings in the district to improve access to justice.

The circuit hearings in Namatanai resumed this week.

Namatanai local level government (LLG) president Elias Towarkia welcomed the restoration of National Court hearings in Namatanai and pledged the provincial government’s support.

“On behalf of the people of Namatanai and New Ireland, I call on the judiciary services to continue from here on,” Towarkia said.

Towarkia also encouraged young people in New Ireland to pursue careers in law and judiciary.

Meanwhile, Justice Mogish stressed that access to courts was a basic human right, noting that many people had to travel four to five hours to Kavieng to access legal services.

“Safety is not guaranteed for people travelling along the Buluminski Highway for four to five  hours to Kavieng to access judiciary services and it’s costly to access these services from Kavieng,” he said.

He also called for the re-establishment of the Correctional Services facilities in Namatanai, saying this would allow detainees to remain closer to their communities.

Work has already begun on a modern courthouse in Namatanai, which is expected to be completed next year.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Ex-Socceroo skipper backs PNG Chiefs. Just smash them, says visiting guest speaker Wade
item.description: FORMER Australian football captain Paul Wade has thrown his support behind the PNG Chiefs ahead of their planned entry into the National Rugby League (NRL) competition in 2028. “Oh, it’s just another team to take all the gloss off Australian Rugby League,” Wade told The National. “They all think they’re the best in the world. Let’s hope Papua New Guinea just smash them. Take away that shine and co
item.content:encoded:
FORMER Australian football captain Paul Wade has thrown his support behind the PNG Chiefs ahead of their planned entry into the National Rugby League (NRL) competition in 2028.

“Oh, it’s just another team to take all the gloss off Australian Rugby League,” Wade told The National. “They all think they’re the best in the world. Let’s hope Papua New Guinea just smash them. Take away that shine and confidence. Let’s hope Papua New Guinea win the competition.”

Wade, the 34th annual SP Sports Awards guest speaker and his partner Velerie, arrived in Port Moresby for the first time yesterday ahead of the awards night set for tomorrow at the Crown Hotel.

The former Socceroo captain also spoke positively about his first experience in Papua New Guinea, brushing off all the negative stories he has heard before arriving in the nation’s capital.

“The first thing they say is everybody’s got a machete and that’s all the stories you hear,” Wade said. “But when I walked out, there was so much joy in these people.

“They are just beautiful people. Thank you for making us feel very welcome. Me and my wife are going to have a very good week here.”

Wade is widely respected in Australian football after an outstanding playing career that saw him represent Australia for more than a decade, and captaining the national side between 1990 and 1996.

Born in England before moving to Australia as a child, Wade became one of the country’s most recognised football figures. He played more than 100 matches for the Socceroos and became famous around the world for marking Argentine legend Diego Maradona during Australia’s 1994 FIFA World Cup qualifying matches against Argentina.

Wade said sport plays an important role in inspiring young people and helping them chase their dreams.

“The biggest problem young people have is they don’t always have heroes they can talk to because many are overseas,” he said.

“I feel very privileged and honoured having done what I’ve done, marking Maradona and representing my country.”

Wade said he hopes his journey can motivate young PNG athletes to believe in themselves.

“If I can mark a Maradona, imagine what you could do,” he said.

“I want to inspire them to be their best so one day they can make their dreams come true.”

During his career, Wade also represented Australia at the 1988 Olympic Games and played in multiple Fifa World Cup qualifying campaigns. He earned praise from Maradona himself after Australia’s famous clashes with Argentina in 1993.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: PIL backs Capital rugby with K250,000
item.description: PAPUA New Guinea’s leading rugby union competition, Capital Rugby Union (CRU), received a quarter million kina in sponsorship from Pacific Industries Limited (PIL), through its iconic Pepsi brand for 2026. CRU president Kori Chan accepted the cheque for K250,000 from PIL director Everett Chue this week in Port Moresby.
item.content:encoded:
PAPUA New Guinea’s leading rugby union competition, Capital Rugby Union (CRU), received a quarter million kina in sponsorship from Pacific Industries Limited (PIL), through its iconic Pepsi brand for 2026.

CRU president Kori Chan accepted the cheque for K250,000 from PIL director Everett Chue this week in Port Moresby.

Chan said the sizable backing reaffirmed the company’s ongoing commitment to the growth and development of rugby union in the nation’s capital and the country.

PIL, through its Pepsi brand, has supported CRU, through partnerships dating back to 2021 and the brand now enters its third consecutive year as the official naming rights sponsor of the CRU’s competition.

The company has remained committed to supporting community sport and youth development initiatives throughout the country, with Pepsi’s continued involvement in CRU reflecting the brand’s passion for empowering local talent, strengthening communities, and promoting healthy and active lifestyles.

Through the sponsorship, Pepsi branding and fan engagement activities will continue to feature prominently throughout the 2026 season, including match-day activations, competition branding, media exposure, merchandise, and community rugby initiatives.

Chue said the company was proud to continue supporting one of Papua New Guinea’s premier rugby union competitions.

“Capital Rugby Union continues to provide an important platform for athletes, clubs, and communities to come together through sport,” he said.

“Through our Pepsi brand, we are proud to continue this partnership into 2026 and contribute towards the continued development of rugby union in PNG.

“At Pacific Industries, we believe in supporting initiatives that inspire communities, encourage participation, and create opportunities for young Papua New Guineans to grow both on and off the field.”

Since becoming naming rights sponsor in 2024, Pepsi’s support has contributed towards strengthening CRU competitions across the men’s, women’s, and junior pathways, while helping create greater exposure and opportunities for emerging rugby talent.

CRU president Chan welcomed the continuation of the sponsorship and acknowledged Pepsi as one of the union’s key long-term partners and allies.

“Pepsi has become an integral partner of CRU, and we are grateful for their continued support heading into another season,” he said.

“Partnerships like this are critical in helping us grow the competition, strengthen our pathways, and continue building rugby union in the nation’s capital.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Southerners travel into enemy lines for round 8
item.description: THE 2026 Digicel ExxonMobil Cup enters Round 8 this weekend, with the Asila Waghi Tumbe hosting PRK Gulf Isou on Saturday at the John Ambane Oval in Jiwaka. Tumbe will be eager to rebound after a 32-20 loss to Kandes in Round 7.
item.content:encoded:
THE 2026 Digicel ExxonMobil Cup enters Round 8 this weekend, with the Asila Waghi Tumbe hosting PRK Gulf Isou on Saturday at the John Ambane Oval in Jiwaka.

Tumbe will be eager to rebound after a 32-20 loss to Kandes in Round 7.

Gulf Isou, also coming off defeat, struggled defensively in their 30-14 loss to the Wamp Nga Mt Hagen Eagles and must tighten up against Tumbe.

But, with a recent morale boosting sponsorship by TotalEnergies, the crocodiles are all fired up for this weekend away from home.

Lae Snax Tigers return home brimming with confidence to face Moni Plus Port Moresby Vipers at the Lae League Ground on Sunday.

The Tigers edged Gas Resource Central Dabaris 20-18 in one of Round 7’s toughest battles, while the Vipers are under pressure after falling 18-12 to Bintangor Goroka Lahanis. The New Porgera Limited Enga Mioks will host Dabaris at Aipus Oval in Enga.

The Mioks impressed with a 24-24 draw against ENB Agmark Gurias, while Dabaris narrowly lost to the Tigers.

Bintangor Goroka Lahanis face competition leaders Kandes RLC at the National Sports Institute in Goroka.

The Lahanis’ defensive grit earned them an 18-12 win over the Vipers, while Kandes continued their rise with a 32-20 victory over Tumbe.

Kroton Hela Wigmen meet ENB Agmark Gurias at Sir John Guise Stadium in Port Moresby.

The Wigmen dominated the Muruks 24-12 last weekend, while the Gurias shared points in a 24-24 draw with the Mioks.

PRK Mendi Muruks battle Wamp Nga Mt Hagen Eagles in the final game at Lae League Ground.

The Muruks seek recovery after their loss to the Wigmen, while the Eagles enter with momentum after defeating Gulf Isou 30-14.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: 2026 Kokoda Cup ready to rumble, Webber set for battle
item.description: THE annual Kokoda Cup boxing show on Sunday will feature competitors as young as 10 and as well as Papua New Guinea’s elite talent taking on a contingent of fighters from New South Wales and Queensland. The bouts are set to take place at the Gold Club in Waigani and the show is a major part of the PNG Boxing Union’s fundraising drive for its development and elite programmes and for its team to the
item.content:encoded:
THE annual Kokoda Cup boxing show on Sunday will feature competitors as young as 10 and as well as Papua New Guinea’s elite talent taking on a contingent of fighters from New South Wales and Queensland.

The bouts are set to take place at the Gold Club in Waigani and the show is a major part of the PNG Boxing Union’s fundraising drive for its development and elite programmes and for its team to the Commonwealth Games in Glasgow, Scotland, in August.

Kicking off the show will be cadet and youth boxers.

Queensland’s Hunter Haumara Webber, 10, will face off against Shadrach Vake in the country’s first ever international 36 kilogramme bout at the Gold club’s Arena.

Hunter is actually part Papua New Guinean by way of Derek Webber whose mother is from Yule Island, Kairuku in Central province.

Hunters will have not just his father in his corner but his mother Kellie Webber, aunts Samantha Webber and Silvia Haurama, and grandfathers Philip Webber and Ian Preston in the crowd.

They form part of the 27-member supporters group from Queensland and eight from New South Wales, comprising parents and family members who will be here to watch the boxers and experience Port Moresby. 

Sean Baker, a 15-year old from Queensland, will meet PNG’s Lazarus Kamali in the youth category (51kg division).

Sean’s father spent some of his younger years in Port Moresby many years back will be part of the travelling party to watch his son fight and visit places he has fond memories of.

Also included in the Queensland team will be state champion Mili Saul, who has pledged her allegiance to PNG to push for selection for Team PNG to Glasgow.

The Milne Bay lass has participated in the last two PNG National Boxing Championships.

Minister for Works and Highways Peter Tsiamalii Jnr is set to be the special guest at the show.

PNGBU president Dr Gideon Kendino said Minister Tsiamalili’s presence was a significant boost to the event. 

PNGBU secretary general Martin Liri said the Kokoda Cup was the union’s main event on their 2026 calendar and always been a great fundraiser and opportunity for exposure and experience for PNG’s best boxers.

“It also promotes the long history between Australia and PNG, reminding us of the relationship that shone brightest during World War Two,” Liri said.

Kendino also acknowledged Tsiamalili’s support of sports in the country which covered rugby union, rugby league, boxing and other codes.

“Queensland has nine boxers while NSW has eight boxers who will meet their PNG opponents in 18 bouts whereby our Commonwealth Games boxers and management will use the opportunity to monitor where our preparations are at,” Kendino said.

“The Kokoda Cup is now a permanent part of their calendar as it not only provides quality international competitions for our boxers but doubles up as a major activity to raise funds for the Union’s various programmes.”

Kendino also acknowledged sponsors Kumul Minerals Holdings Ltd, MVIL, Road Traffic Authourity, National Finance, Pacific Racing, South Pacific Motors Sports Club, Lamana Hotel, Time Travel Pacific, National Capital District Commission, Adventure Kokoda, Sesago Discount Pharmacy as well as partners PNG Olympic Committee and PNG Sports Foundation.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Local control, transparency in revenues
item.description: LANDOWNERS and provincial governments should have direct management control of the 4.27 per cent Kroton Equity Special Purpose Vehicle (SPV) because it concerns resources extracted from their own land. For many Papua New Guineans, this issue goes beyond politics and speaks directly to fairness, ownership and self-determination.
item.content:encoded:
LANDOWNERS and provincial governments should have direct management control of the 4.27 per cent Kroton Equity Special Purpose Vehicle (SPV) because it concerns resources extracted from their own land.

For many Papua New Guineans, this issue goes beyond politics and speaks directly to fairness, ownership and self-determination.

For too long, the benefits from the PNG Liquefied Natural Gas Project have been managed through centralised systems that communities believe have failed to deliver meaningful development outcomes.

Former prime minister Peter O’Neill’s recent intervention in Parliament has reignited an important national debate about transparency, accountability and who should control resource benefits intended for landowners and impacted provinces.

If the purpose of the SPV is to deliver tangible benefits to resource owners, then landowners and provincial governments must play the leading role in managing and overseeing the fund.

Late governor Anderson Agiru consistently argued that excessive political interference would undermine both public trust and the integrity of the equity arrangement.

His position reflected the view that development decisions should be driven by the people most affected by resource extraction.

There are also growing public calls for a full report into the more than K12 billion earned through the equity stake since production began in 2014.

Transparency is essential to restoring confidence and ensuring revenues are properly accounted for.

Ultimately, this debate is about empowering communities, strengthening accountability, and ensuring PNG’s resource wealth delivers lasting benefits for future generations.

THE recent parliamentary call by former prime minister Peter O’Neill for a full report into more than K12 billion in PNG Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG) Project revenues deserves bipartisan support from all members of Parliament, especially governors and MPs from the Papua region.

This issue must rise above politics, personalities and leadership rivalries.

It concerns transparency, accountability and the responsible management of Papua New Guinea’s resource wealth.

Prime Minister James Marape’s commitment to table a comprehensive report is timely and necessary, and Parliament must now engage in a mature and constructive debate focused on facts, lessons and reforms.

Papua MPs, in particular, should not remain silent on matters affecting landowners, impacted communities and future generations connected to the PNG LNG Project.

The people expect leadership that is courageous, responsible and guided by the national interest.

The debate should not become a platform for political attacks or personal vendettas.

Transparency and accountability strengthen democratic institutions and build public trust.

As Parliament examines the past, it must also focus on creating fair, transparent and sustainable benefit-sharing arrangements for future LNG and petroleum projects to ensure all stakeholders benefit equitably from the nation’s resources.

Reginald Renagi Strategic leadership and governance advisor–advocate

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Manuals to guide provincial development
item.description: THE recent launching of the seven-volume Financial Management Manual by Department of Finance endorsed by National Executive Council is a fundamental guideline to manage various aspects of public finance. These series of manuals are important for users in sub-national systems, government departments, statutory bodies and State-owned enterprises.
item.content:encoded:
THE recent launching of the seven-volume Financial Management Manual by Department of Finance endorsed by National Executive Council is a fundamental guideline to manage various aspects of public finance.

These series of manuals are important for users in sub-national systems, government departments, statutory bodies and State-owned enterprises.

These State agencies are directly responsible for the procurement and spending of public funds in delivery of basic goods and services.

The establishment of Audit Committees in these entities should ensure that standard practices of procuring expenditure should be adhered to in the application of these public monies for tangible impact to change the way of life of the local people where they are domiciled.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Oral cancers linked to betel nut chewing
item.description: DR Xu Wei, thoracic surgeon and team leader of the 15th China medical team to Papua New Guinea, has warned Papua New Guineans about the serious health dangers of betel nut chewing. His message should be taken seriously, not as criticism of culture, but as a call to protect lives and improve public health.
item.content:encoded:
DR Xu Wei, thoracic surgeon and team leader of the 15th China medical team to Papua New Guinea, has warned Papua New Guineans about the serious health dangers of betel nut chewing.

His message should be taken seriously, not as criticism of culture, but as a call to protect lives and improve public health.

Betel nut is classified by the International Agency for Research on Cancer as a Group 1 carcinogen, meaning there is clear evidence it causes cancer in humans.

 In PNG, an estimated 84 per cent of oral and pharyngeal cancer cases are linked to betel nut chewing.

Xu explains that oral cancer can be devastating, often requiring the removal of parts of the mouth, tongue, or jaw, leaving patients unable to eat or speak normally.

The risk becomes even greater when betel nut is combined with smoking.

Beyond cancer, betel nut contributes to gum disease, tooth loss, poor hygiene and the spread of diseases such as tuberculosis.

PNG already faces some of the world’s highest oral cancer rates.

Quitting betel nut can save lives, reduce pressure on hospitals and help build a healthier future for the country.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: True leadership in action
item.description: A NEWS headline, ‘Governor Pays K300,000 Owed Monies’, published in one of the daily newspapers yesterday revealed that the outstanding debt was not incurred under the current administration, but by the previous government. For Eastern Highlands Governor Simon Sia to settle bills left unpaid by his predecessor is a commendable act of leadership and generosity.
item.content:encoded:
A NEWS headline, ‘Governor Pays K300,000 Owed Monies’, published in one of the daily newspapers yesterday revealed that the outstanding debt was not incurred under the current administration, but by the previous government.

For Eastern Highlands Governor Simon Sia to settle bills left unpaid by his predecessor is a commendable act of leadership and generosity.

Many leaders would be reluctant to take responsibility for obligations they did not create, especially those linked to political opponents.

Sia’s actions demonstrate maturity, unity, and a genuine commitment to serving the people of Eastern Highlands above political differences.

It also reflects his ability to manage provincial resources with discipline and responsibility.

Service providers who were previously let down can now regain confidence through this gesture.

Sia deserves recognition for showing the qualities of a true and genuine leader by putting the interests of the province and its people first.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Pacific concerns about militarisation and NZ’s role in it
item.description: NEW Zealand’s government is increasingly eager to promote the buy-in of Pacific nations for closer defence force integration in the region, amid concerns about militarisation of the region. The security environment has been shifting rapidly, and regional defence is becoming more complex, leaving Pacific Islanders wondering if their “Ocean of Peace” is slipping out of their grasp.
item.content:encoded:
NEW Zealand’s government is increasingly eager to promote the buy-in of Pacific nations for closer defence force integration in the region, amid concerns about militarisation of the region.

The security environment has been shifting rapidly, and regional defence is becoming more complex, leaving Pacific Islanders wondering if their “Ocean of Peace” is slipping out of their grasp.

In recent months, the defence and police forces of Australia and New Zealand have been increasing cooperation with counterparts in Pacific countries, including Fiji, Tonga, Samoa and Vanuatu in efforts to combat transnational crime, especially the illicit drug trade. But as a number of Pacific Island countries weigh up signing major bilateral treaties or agreements with the likes of Australia, China and the United States (US), New Zealand has been steadily pushing Pacific regional defence cooperation on a number of fronts.

The communiqué from last October’s South Pacific Defence Ministers Meeting (SPDMM) in Chile is instructive.

The SPDMM – which involves New Zealand, Australia, Chile, Fiji, France, Papua New Guinea and Tonga – notes the leadership role New Zealand has taken on better coordinating regional defence architecture.

The member countries agreed to push for a defence advisor from their collective to be embedded in the secretariat of the Pacific Islands Forum (PIF), whose secretary-general Baron Waqa attended the Chile meeting and appeared to support closer integration.

While the advisor position is yet to be established, the SPDMM is surging ahead with a range of new regional defence initiatives, including developing the Pacific Response Group, under which defence personnel from Australia, Fiji, France, New Zealand and PNG work together to support coordinated humanitarian assistance and disaster relief responses across the Pacific.

A briefing from March’s joint meeting of the defence and foreign ministers of Australia and New Zealand, emphasised how they aim to promote “the sense of integration through Pacific defence forces’ and to ‘enhance the sense of Pacific forces meeting Pacific security needs”.

It also highlighted a keenness to “get more links between SPDMM and PIF so that these voices are heard directly by the region”.

“The briefings reveal New Zealand’s role in integrating and aligning Pacific defence forces alongside a considerable anxiety about regional social licence,” Pacific historian Marco de Jong said.

He said the language being used “speaks to a programme of influence and public relations, calibrated to downplay criticism that New Zealand is contributing to the militarisation of the Pacific”.

A representative with Development Alternatives with Women for a New Era, Maureen Penjueli, who is also a long time advocate for indigenous rights in the region, said there had been a lack of consultation with the wider Pacific Islands region about the new defence tack.

“We’ve seen so much occupation by those in the defence interests area. For example, the Australian National Security College takes a very primary seat at the Pacific Islands Forum on security. We’ve got competing interests, which is the Fusion Centre that’s in Vanuatu,” Penjueli said.

“When you add more and more players to this regional architecture that already has enough players on defence and security, it complicates the governance structure in a way.

“It does not go through the rigour of national consultations, consultations with civil society around some of these bigger significant shifts around defence and security.”

New Zealand is a long-standing contributor to Pacific regional initiatives, and its defence force is well valued in the region, especially in responding to disasters, humanitarian needs, transnational crime and maritime security threats, and also including in training support.

New Zealand’s defence minister Chris Penk, who replaced Judith Collins in the role since March’s 2+2 Ministerial Meeting with Australia, said that New Zealand always sought to adapt its work with Pacific partners to their context, culture and operational needs.

He told RNZ that in order to support closer cooperation between Pacific militaries, members were also looking at a SPDMM Status of Forces Agreement.

“This would provide a common legal framework for personnel to deploy into each other’s countries more easily, strengthening our collective ability to respond to maritime security challenges as well as humanitarian and disaster relief events.”

Asked if New Zealand is contributing to militarisation of the Pacific Islands, Penk said Pacific partners had warmly welcomed the country’s continued presence and partnership in the region

“The New Zealand defence force contributes to regional responses where it is agreed that defence force personnel and assets should be involved, including humanitarian assistance, maritime domain awareness, fisheries patrols, and search and rescue operations.

“As a Pacific military, we are proud to work alongside our Pacific partners to help respond to the challenges facing our region.”

Penjueli warned that militarisation of the region was escalating against the wishes of most Pacific Islands people.

Making things more complicated, she said, was the growing number of security treaties and agreements that Island countries were being drawn to.

She said they were no longer just about defence or security inter-operability, and often included development and economic dimensions, arrangements that “entangled” Pacific countries into wide ranging commitments beyond traditional military and security ties.

Penjueli worried that the interests of the Islands countries themselves were more than ever being buried under broader geopolitical jostling.

“We were told that this is to ready the region in an anticipation, to contain China, and we’re told that this is about the drug trade and the drug war that’s taking place.

“Yet for the Pacific, climate change or the climate crisis, remains our significant issue around security. So, I think the agendas are very different.”

At their last leaders summit, PIF countries signed up to the Blue Pacific Ocean of Peace Declaration, formally committing the region to peace, sovereignty, and climate justice.

However, Penjueli said being a true ocean of peace required demilitarisation and de-escalation – something which she suggested is not the direction that the defence-oriented governments of the region are heading in. – RNZ

item.title: Ex-security guard Zeming graduates with diploma
item.description: THANKS to young sister Penelope who had challenged family members to pursue further studies and graduate from tertiary institutions, Zeming Zeming received not only a Diploma in Business Management but also the top-student award at the International Training Institute graduation on Friday. “In my immediate family, only the two of us have graduated – me and my small sister (Penelope). She had
item.content:encoded:
THANKS to young sister Penelope who had challenged family members to pursue further studies and graduate from tertiary institutions, Zeming Zeming received not only a Diploma in Business Management but also the top-student award at the International Training Institute graduation on Friday.

“In my immediate family, only the two of us have graduated – me and my small sister (Penelope). She had graduated earlier (in 2022) and she used to challenge us to obtain qualifications (from tertiary institutions) as well.”

Zeming, 31, of Zankoa village in Tewae-Siassi district, Morobe used to score good marks in primary school but never became a top student or dux.

“I was never a dux. This is my first time being a dux. I thank God and my family for this.”

Zeming is the third eldest of five siblings – two brothers and three sisters. Their father Mao Zeming is self-employed while Mum Fredah died in 2018.

Zeming did not complete school because of financial issues. So he took on informal jobs to enroll in the diploma course at ITI.

“I didn’t complete my schooling due to school fee issues. I looked for jobs instead. I became a bus driver, security guard and all sorts of informal jobs until this opportunity arose and I grabbed it with both hands.”

His job as a security guard kindled his interest in management.

“I worked as a security and was happy to interact with people. I felt like I could manage their small complaints and issues. It got me interested in the management course.”

He faced challenges during the two years of study.

“The first two years were not easy. I tried my best to go to work and attend classes. And in the end, I achieved this.”

His family motivated him to get his diploma.

“We are a close-knit family. Every time my brother would counsel me. He reminded me of our humble beginnings and not to take education for granted. It was their motivation that kept me going.”

His father’s strict rules on time guided his study routine.

“ I didn’t complete my schooling due to school fee issues. I looked for jobs instead. I became a bus driver, security guard and all sorts of informal jobs until this opportunity arose and I grabbed it with both hands.”

“Focus is one thing that my father talks about every time, reminding us to be early before schedule and all that. He is a very strict man, very strict on time.

“Some things we don’t know about, he encourages us to ask people for help, to be curious. So yes, it was my father’s strictness that had shaped my study routine.”

“I always make sure that I did my homework and my studies and that things are in order.

“The good thing is that, I have a good family. They are very understanding people. Once in a while they would ask me for help. But mostly, they respect me; they played their part and mine as well.”

“My brother is always my role model. He used to remind us that success is there, all you need to do to achieve it is to remain focused.

“He does not have a big job but he ensures that we all are taken care of. He took on the family responsibility and I have respect for him.”

Zeming is happy to have accomplished his goal.

“I am really happy. Seeing (cousin sister) Luddy also graduating today makes me really happy. We helped each other at school. We also help other Morobe students as well and in the end, we made it.”

Zeming dedicated the dux award to his late mother and his diploma to his father.

“I dedicated these achievements to my family, those who had helped me with my school fees and my upbringing. And this dux award is dedicated to my late mum. Dad gets the diploma.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Neidab dedicates degree, award to late father
item.description: WHEN Neidab Ulu went on stage to receive his degree and award, he was oblivious to the cheering, applause and claps as he was missing and thinking of the person he wanted to be there to witness his achievement – his late father. “I lost my father during the first semester last year. He should have been attending and witnessing this ceremony.”
item.content:encoded:
WHEN Neidab Ulu went on stage to receive his degree and award, he was oblivious to the cheering, applause and claps as he was missing and thinking of the person he wanted to be there to witness his achievement – his late father.

“I lost my father during the first semester last year. He should have been attending and witnessing this ceremony.”

Neidab lost his father Ulu Rusini last year during the second semester of his final year – a critical time for any final year university student.

However, he managed to pull through and graduated with a Bachelor in Public Finance and Accountancy degree.

Neidab, 39, from Wanigela Village, Abau District, Central, is the second eldest of six children.

He was among those who graduated from the University of PNG School of Business and Public Policy last Thursday.

To his employer, the Public Service Commission, he was an investment which paid off when he not only graduated but also named the top performing student in the Bachelor of Public Finance and Accountancy class.

He received the Academic Excellence Award after being sponsored by the PSC, where he has worked for 11 years, to pursue the degree while working as the acting Senior Budget Officer and Certifying Officer.

It was an opportunity he took seriously.

“I thank my employer, the Public Service Commission, for the investment and opportunity and trust placed in me to send me for further studies.”

His work involves budget support, expenditure management, and financial advisory duties. He knows that the degree will help him in his work and lift his performance.

“What motivated me to go back for further studies was to enhance my skills in my current duties on the processes in place to manage the public funds effectively and efficiently.”

Neidab attained a Certificate in Business Studies in 2007 and a Diploma in Business Studies in 2008 at the Port Moresby Business College.

In 2023, he graduated with a Diploma in Accounting from the Somare Institute of Leadership and Governance (SILAG).

The loss of his father affected him. On top of that, he faced financial issues and tried his best to juggle his time between work, study, and family.

“I encountered financial challenges attending classes, attending to family matters, as well as work.”

At times when work and study demanded his attention most, he prioritised which one to tackle first.

“I’ve had clashes between my studies and my job. Some time, I had to attend to my work first and then my studies second because work is my bread and butter.”

The experience changed how he managed his time.

“It shaped me as a person to think critically and handle issues in a way that is balanced.”

At home, his wife Wendy Ulu and two sons Neidrolson who is in Grade Six, and Braden in Grade 4, endured nights when he had to burn the midnight oil.

Wife Wendy remembers those nights when they hardly went to sleep as Neidab would spend hours studying and keeping the lights on.

So she was happy and relieved to see him graduate with flying colours – a fitting reward for his hard work and perseverance. For the family, the hours of struggling to sleep are finally over.

Neidab dedicated the award to his employer, his late father, and his family.

“I dedicate the award to my employer and to my father who should have attended and witnessed this ceremony. And not forgetting my family who supported me throughout this journey.”
